summary: This early blog post reflects on the invisible craftsmanship behind well-designed tools. Shapiro (via contributing editor Brian Todoroff) argues that good designers agonize over countless small details that most users never consciously notice. The post uses a woodworking depth stop as a case study, featuring designer Brian Giffin's explanation of why he made specific material and geometry choices. The conclusion is an appreciation for the unseen engineering effort that makes everyday tools work seamlessly.
key_points: The best tools become invisible through good design — users never think about why they work so well
Good designers obsess over details like handle length, switch placement, and material choice, spending far more thought on each aspect than users ever will
Brian Giffin's depth stop design illustrates thoughtful engineering: convex shape for angled drilling, brass for heat dissipation, and chip relief for consistent depth
The 80% solution philosophy — imperfect but meaningfully better than nothing — reflects practical design thinking
money_quotes: In some dark corner that you tell no one about is also that colossal waste of money. Perhaps it has long since been forcefully ejected from your sawdust kingdom, but the memory of the wonderful project it ruined still haunts you.
How did they know to add that little curve to give your thumb just enough clearance? How did they come up with the million other details that make everything come together so smoothly?
In most cases they put infinitely more thought into each little aspect of the design then most of us ever put into using the tool.
I made it convex so that it contacts the inner part first, even when slightly angled.
It's not a perfect solution but 80% seemed better than nothing.
key_insight: The best product design is invisible — great tools work so well because their designers obsessed over details that users will never consciously notice.
